summary: Joel discusses his effort to maintain translations of Joel on Software, noting 264 translations in progress across 32 languages with 242 volunteers. He asks for copy editors in 15 languages to review completed translations before posting. He addresses why translations matter even for a programming blog, arguing that not all programmers are fluent in English and that their non-technical managers may not read English at all. He also demonstrates why machine translation is inadequate by showing a hilariously garbled Google-translated passage.
key_points: Joel on Software had 264 translations in progress across 32 languages, reflecting the blog's significant global reach
The assumption that 'all programmers know English' is wrong — many don't know it well enough to enjoy reading in it, and their managers may not know it at all
Machine translation tools of the era (Babelfish, Google Language Tools) produced output so poor it was essentially incomprehensible
Community-driven translation with volunteer copy editors was the only viable approach for quality multilingual content
money_quotes: Surely any real programmer knows English!
Not every programmer knows English, and if they do, they may not know it that well, so they may not really enjoy reading things written in English if they don't have to.
Even if the programmers have learned enough English to decipher online documentation, their pointy-haired bosses from management may not have.
They are seriously little. You cannot include/understand simply the exit.
The quality of translations produced by automatic software is so horrible that you really can't understand the output.
Then why does nobody make planning? Two principal reasons. Firstly, it is really difficult. Secondly, nobody believes that that is worth the sorrow of it.
key_insight: Quality software writing needs human translation because the audience extends beyond English-fluent programmers to their non-technical managers, and machine translation was nowhere near adequate.
